title: Centella capensis
what_description: Centella capensis is an annual or perennial herbaceous plant belonging to the family Apiaceae. It has palmately lobed, crenate leaves that are alternate and pinnate, with leaflets typically 3-5 in number. The plant produces small white or pinkish flowers in umbels, with 5-7 rays and 5-7 flowers per ray, and is dioecious, meaning the male and female flowers are separate. The fruit is a schizocarp, consisting of two separate mericarps, each containing a single seed. Centella capensis is native to South Africa and is also known as lion's tail or wild potato.
ailments_description: The most common ailments you can heal with Centella capensis are memory loss, which has been improved in individuals with cognitive impairment through its triterpenoid saponin content. Anxiety has been alleviated in animal studies due to the anxiolytic effects of triterpenoid saponins. Diabetes management has been enhanced by the antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties of centella capensis, which reduce oxidative stress and inflammation associated with the disease. Centella capensis has been shown to lower blood pressure and prevent the onset of hypertension in animal models through its flavonoid and triterpenoid saponin content. Additionally, it has been used to treat eczema, inflammation, wound healing, fatigue, Alzheimer's disease, skin inflammation, gastrointestinal issues, Parkinson's disease, skin conditions, asthma, stress relief, digestive issues, muscle spasms, depression, psoriasis, and dementia through its antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and anti-apoptotic properties.
properties_description: The main therapeutic properties of Centella capensis are antioxidant, with flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids contributing to its ability to neutralize free radicals and prevent oxidative stress. This plant's anti-inflammatory properties, attributed to saponins and terpenoids, help to reduce inflammation and alleviate conditions such as arthritis and wounds. Additionally, Centella capensis exhibits antimicrobial properties, with volatile oils and other bioactive compounds inhibiting the growth of bacteria and fungi, making it effective against infections. Furthermore, the neuroprotective properties of Centella capensis, attributed to flavonoids and phenolic acids, may help to prevent or slow the progression of neurodegenerative diseases. The plant's wound healing properties, facilitated by saponins and terpenoids, accelerate tissue repair and promote the growth of new skin cells.
constituents_description: The most important healing constituents of Centella capensis are triterpene saponins, oleanolic acid, ursolic acid, asiatic acid, and triterpenoids, which possess potent anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. Oleanolic acid and ursolic acid have been shown to exhibit immunomodulatory and hepatoprotective effects, while asiatic acid has been found to have neuroprotective properties. Triterpene saponins, including asiaticoside, have been demonstrated to accelerate wound healing and improve cognitive function. Asiatic acid has also been reported to inhibit nitric oxide production and reduce inflammation. These constituents contribute to the plant's therapeutic effects, which have been traditionally used to treat various conditions, including skin ulcers, eczema, and neurodegenerative diseases.
preparations_description: The main herbal preparations of Centella capensis are derived from its leaves and stems, which contain triterpenoid saponins and flavonoids. A tincture is made by soaking the plant material in a solvent such as ethanol to create a concentrated liquid extract, which can be used to treat various health issues. An infusion or tea is prepared by steeping the dried leaves in hot water to release the bioactive compounds, which may help to soothe skin irritations and wounds. A salve or topical cream is created by infusing the plant material in a carrier oil, such as coconut or olive oil, to treat skin conditions and promote wound healing. Capsules are filled with dried and powdered Centella capensis leaves, which can be taken orally to support cognitive function and improve memory.
side_effects_description: The possible side effects of using Centella capensis improperly include experiencing nausea due to its saponin and triterpenoid content, which can irritate the stomach and cause gastrointestinal discomfort. This plant has also been linked to hepatotoxicity, specifically liver damage, resulting from its high concentration of triterpenoids, which can induce oxidative stress and disrupt liver function. Furthermore, excessive consumption of Centella capensis can lead to skin irritation, such as rashes and dermatitis, due to its high saponin content, which can cause skin inflammation and allergic reactions. Additionally, high doses of Centella capensis have been reported to cause seizures in some individuals, likely due to its neurotoxic effects, which can disrupt normal neurotransmitter function and lead to convulsions. These adverse reactions highlight the importance of proper dosing and consultation with a healthcare professional when using Centella capensis for medicinal purposes.
uses: Centella capensis, a plant native to South Africa, has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, particularly in the treatment of skin conditions and wounds. It is said to possess anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, making it useful in the management of conditions such as eczema and acne. The plant's extracts have also been used to promote wound healing and reduce the risk of infection. Additionally, Centella capensis is believed to have antioxidant properties, which can help protect the body against oxidative stress and cell damage. Further research is needed to fully understand the plant's potential medicinal uses and benefits.
taxonomy: Centella capensis is classified within the family Hydrocotylaceae and the genus Centella. It is a member of the plant family Hydrocotylaceae, which is a group of aquatic plants. The genus Centella, to which Centella capensis belongs, is a group of plants that are commonly known as the "gotu kola" plants. This classification places Centella capensis among other plants with similar characteristics, such as aquatic growth habits and leafy foliage. The specific classification of Centella capensis reflects its unique position within the plant kingdom.

intro: Centella capensis, also known as Lion's Tail or Kalmer, is a perennial herb belonging to the Apiaceae family, native to southern Africa. It has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, with health benefits including improved cognitive function, wound healing, and enhanced cardiovascular health due to its anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. The bioactive constituents of Centella capensis include triterpenoid saponins, flavonoids, and alkaloids, which contribute to its therapeutic effects. The most common herbal preparations made with Centella capensis are teas, tinctures, and capsules, often used to support brain function, memory, and overall well-being.
